bull.b: Oracle’s business is anchored in high-quality recurring revenue—subscriptions and support for databases, applications and cloud. This installed-base renewal model drives predictable cash flows, high customer stickiness, and durable upsell pathways into higher‑margin cloud and AI services.

bull.t: Durable Revenue Growth And Cloud Momentum
bull.b: Multi-year top-line expansion reflects successful migration of customers to cloud and expansion into AI workloads. Sustained revenue growth indicates structural demand for Oracle’s database, SaaS and OCI offerings and supports long‑term operating leverage as cloud mix increases.

bull.t: Strong Forward Visibility Via RPO And Operating Cash Flow
bull.b: A very large RPO backlog provides multi‑year revenue visibility and de‑risked demand for capacity. Coupled with materially improved operating cash flow historically, this backlog supports capacity planning, customer retention and long‑term revenue conversion once investments reach steady state.

bear.b: Debt has increased materially, leaving Oracle with a debt‑heavy capital structure and elevated leverage ratios. High leverage reduces financial flexibility, raises refinancing and interest‑rate exposure, and constrains the company’s ability to absorb shocks or pursue opportunistic investments without further financing.

Despite stronger operating cash flow, FCF turned deeply negative after heavy investment. Negative FCF implies cash consumed by CapEx and timing effects, increasing reliance on external financing and making earnings less convertible to shareholder returns until investments stabilize.

bear.t: Very Large CapEx And Financing Needs
bear.b: Aggressive data‑center buildout requires very large near‑term capital and execution; management plans material debt and equity raises. This creates margin pressure during ramp, execution and dilution risk, and increases dependence on projected utilization and contract economics to justify long‑term returns.
